course_title: The Political Dynamics of Development and Resistance

learning_outcomes.description: Appreciate the relationship between theory and practice in development.
Competently analyse political dynamics in development processes at local, regional and global levels, as well as between them.
Successfully relate issues and problems in contemporary development governance to their historical, social, and political underpinnings.
Identify and critically analyse conceptual frameworks in the context of case examples.

assessments.task_description:
You will receive 2 marks for attending and participating actively in seminar proceedings.

It is your responsibility to sign off on the weekly attendance list, which will be circulated during each seminar.

Please note that there are no alternative make-up arrangements for this assessment item. Should you miss a class due to illness, you will receive the mark if there is a doctor's certificate covering the instance. For exceptional circumstances (e.g. family emergencies), please contact the course convenor.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
You will write a research essay of 2000 words (excluding reference list) on a topic you can choose from a list of questions provided during the early weeks of the course.

To successfully complete this assignment, prepare and submit your research essay (via Turnitin) by the specified due date.

Please note: You should have a full draft of your essay ready two weeks before submission to give yourself time to reflect on and improve your submission.

The essay needs to clearly state the essay question up front. The layout should use double-spacing. The word-count needs to be provided at the end of the essay's text, before the reference list. You are required to engage with the mandatory and recommended readings from the course reading list as relevant for your question in addition to any other sources you may wish to consult.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assignments for this course (Essay) will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using Turnitin.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
You are required to answer two questions from a list of options. The answers should be in the format of short essays and planned for a well-structured and analytically compelling response. Please ensure that you write clearly and express yourself purposefully throughout.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Welcome and Introductions

Our first meeting will be dedicated to introducing and outlining the course, its modalities and its objectives. We will talk about the organization of the learning experience, and about how we will approach the complex but very interesting topic of political dynamics of development and resistance . To prepare for the opening meeting, please read Chapter 1 from Development and Social Change (7th edition), which you can access through the Library Reading List for POLS2404.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 1: Welcome and Introductions Our first meeting will be dedicated to introducing and outlining the course, its modalities and its objectives. We will talk about the organization of the learning experience, and about how we will approach the complex but very interesting topic of  political dynamics of development and resistance . To prepare for the opening meeting, please read Chapter 1 from Development and Social Change (7th edition), which you can access through the Library Reading List for POLS2404.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2: Analysing Politics of Development & Resistance

This week s session will focus on introducing and discussing broad conceptual and analytical challenges in studying the politics of development, and how these can be met. Against the backdrop of an outline of historical lineages underpinning contemporary political constellations and contestations around development, we will explore conventional and alternative ideas about development. We discuss the the problem of 'the object of development' by focusing on the implications of state-centric politics and policy. Relational analysis will be introduced and discussed with the help of examples and contextualised with how we approach the course.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Week 3: EKKA Public Holiday

No Lecture or Seminar held in this week.
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 3: EKKA Public Holiday No Lecture or Seminar held in this week.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4: Development contested: a 'double movement'

This week we continue with the task of exploring conceptual aspects of understanding the politics of development and resistance, and we focus in particular on its political economy underpinnings. Karl Polanyi s work provides a rich and instructive framework for working through some of the contradictions and conflicts induced by market-centred development. We encounter his concept of a double-movement and consider its relevance for understanding and analysing resistance to the politics of the market . Some concrete examples will supplement our discussion throughout.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

We encounter his concept of a  double-movement  and consider its relevance for understanding and analysing resistance to the  politics of the market . Some concrete examples will supplement our discussion throughout.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5: Colonialism, anti-colonial struggles, and the politics of knowledge

In this session, we trace contemporary concerns with international and transnational inequality and political domination to how these were co-constituted through colonialism. We look at anti-colonial struggles, their successes and challenges, and link these accounts to questions of knowledge, the politics of representation, and the injustices engendered.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 5: Colonialism, anti-colonial struggles, and the politics of knowledge

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Inequality, labour struggles

This week we consider the challenges faced by workers and labourers in the context of the politics of development. The session picks up on our exploration of analyses of political economy and the historical legacies underpinning trends towards social exclusion more generally. Labour-centred struggles and movements against social exclusion have taken different forms, and we explore some contemporary examples based on the analytic developed in the opening sessions. Should struggles centred on labour rights be expanded to questions of a universal basic income and social protection more generally?

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7: Development and Human Rights

In this session, we examine the ways in which human rights and development are being linked. We focus on human rights claiming as a medium through which development and the insecurities and inequalities it comprises are contested.

Learning outcomes: L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Week 7: Development and Human Rights In this session, we examine the ways in which human rights and development are being linked. We focus on human rights claiming as a medium through which development and the insecurities and inequalities it comprises are contested.

learning_activities.topic: 7: Development and Human Rights
learning_activities.topic_description: 7: Development and Human Rights

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Contesting Power through Theatre

Under this topic we focus again on the politics of knowledge in development. The example of the Theatre of the Oppressed shows how drama is used in communities to allow people to better understand and challenge unequal relations of development.As the example shows, such knowledge can be transformative and bring about positive changes that elude official development schemes, or are sometimes even prevented by these.

Learning outcomes: L03, L04

The example of the Theatre of the Oppressed shows how drama is used in communities to allow people to better understand and challenge unequal relations of development.As the example shows, such knowledge can be transformative and bring about positive changes that elude official development schemes, or are sometimes even prevented by these.

8: Contesting Power through Theatre

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: Big Issue- The Landless Movement in Brazil

This week s big issue focusses on a long-standing movement in Brazil that has reversed the widely accepted logic of seeing the city as the domain of emancipated modern life . The landless movement in Brazil has instead focused on bringing populations back to the country-side into communities built around agroecological approaches to farming. We look at the forms of political organization the movement has used to track an alternative development path, and how this contrasts with prominent claims about hunger, food security and 'food availability'.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10: Big Issue:- Indigenous Struggles over Water

In many different parts of the world, indigenous peoples movements have been at the forefront of struggles over preserving and maintaining waterways and the ecological integrity of their lands. We take a closer look at examples of such struggles, and at how the politics of development plays out in such contexts. As environmental concerns become generally more pervasively acknowledged, the indigenous approaches and knowledges regarding water, land and species articulate powerful challenges to conventional development thinking and practice.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11: Big Issue: Pacific Struggles and the Politics of Ecological Change

In this session, we will look at ecological and climate change with a focus on Pacific communities. We will examine contestation over historical responsibilities and contemporary strategies aimed at addressing contemporary injustices.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_title: Week 11: Big Issue: Pacific Struggles and the Politics of Ecological Change

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: Buen Vivir/ Sumak Kawsay-Indigenous Conceptions of Development

In this session, we look at examples from the Latin American context where indigenous struggles challenge conventional approaches to development. In particular we look at the tensions and possibilities this produces, including changes to national constitutions to include the rights of nature.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Outlook and Review

This week will entail a recap of key themes, concepts and issues covered throughout the course. This is an important session as we will collectively reflect on on the learning experiences and how this has informed your outlook going forward.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
